title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Six: In which Howl expresses his feelings with green slime (Fragment 2)
text:
Sophie sewed buttons on Michael’s shirts and listened to Howl going through a spell with Michael. “I know I’m slapdash,” he was saying, “but there’s no need for you to copy me. Always read it right through, carefully, first. The shape of it should tell you a lot, whether it’s self-fulfilling, or self-discovering, or simple incantation, or mixed action and speech. When you’ve decided that, go through again and decide which bits mean what they say and which bits are put as a puzzle. You’re getting on to the more powerful kinds now. You’ll find every spell of power has at least one deliberate mistake or mystery in it to prevent accidents. You have to spot those. Now take this spell …”

Listening to Michael’s halting replies to Howl’s questions, and watching Howl scribble remarks on the paper with a strange, everlasting quill pen, Sophie realized that she could learn a lot too. It dawned on her that if Martha could discover the spell to swap herself and Lettie about at Mrs. Fairfax’s, then she ought to be able to do the same here. With a bit of luck, there might be no need to rely on Calcifer.

When Howl was satisfied that Michael had forgotten all about how much or little he charged people in Porthaven, he took him out into the yard to help with the King’s spell. Sophie creaked to her feet and hobbled to the bench. The spell was clear enough, but Howl’s scrawled remarks defeated her. “I’ve never seen such writing!” she grumbled to the human skull. “Does he use a pen or a poker?” She sorted eagerly through every scrap of paper on the bench and examined the powders and liquids in the crooked jars. “Yes, let’s admit it,” she told the skull. “I snoop. And I have my proper reward. I can find out how to cure fowl pest and abate whooping cough, raise a wind and remove hairs from the face. If Martha had found this lot, she’d still be at Mrs. Fairfax’s.”

Howl, it seemed to Sophie, went and examined all the things she had moved when he came in from the yard. But that seemed to be only restlessness. He seemed not to know what to do with himself after that. Sophie heard him roving up and down during the night. He was only an hour in the bathroom the next morning. He seemed not to be able to contain himself while Michael put on his best plum velvet suit, ready to go to the Palace in Kingsbury, and the two of them wrapped the bulky spell up in golden paper. The spell must have been surprisingly light for its size. Michael could carry it on his own easily, with both his arms wrapped round it. Howl turned the knob over the door red-down for him and sent him out into the street among the painted houses.

“They’re expecting it,” Howl said. “You should only have to wait most of the morning. Tell them a child could work it. Show them. And when you come back, I’ll have a spell of power for you to get to work on. So long.”

He shut the door and roved round the room again. “My feet itch,” he said suddenly. “I’m going for a walk on the hills. Tell Michael the spell I promised him is on the bench. And here’s for you to keep busy with.”

Sophie found a gray-and-scarlet suit, as fancy as the blue-and-silver one, dropped into her lap from nowhere. Howl meanwhile picked up his guitar from its corner, turned the doorknob green-down, and stepped out among the scudding heather above Market Chipping.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Six: In which Howl expresses his feelings with green slime

vocabulary.definition: To become less strong or decrease in intensity.
vocabulary.example: The storm abated by evening.

vocabulary.definition: To encourage or assist someone to do something wrong, especially a crime.
vocabulary.example: He was charged with aiding and abetting the criminals.

vocabulary.definition: Too large and taking up too much space.
vocabulary.example: He carried a bulky suitcase.

vocabulary.definition: To keep one's emotions or behaviour under control.
vocabulary.example: She could barely contain herself with excitement.

vocabulary.definition: To make a sharp, high noise when something moves or is moved.
vocabulary.example: The old floorboards creaked underfoot.

vocabulary.definition: Not straight or aligned; bent or twisted out of shape.
vocabulary.example: He had a crooked smile.

vocabulary.definition: To become clear or obvious (used of a realization).
vocabulary.example: It finally dawned on me that I was wrong.

vocabulary.definition: Done intentionally and carefully planned.
vocabulary.example: There was a deliberate attempt to mislead the public.

vocabulary.definition: Continuing forever or for a very long time without stopping.
vocabulary.example: They swore everlasting friendship.

vocabulary.definition: Speaking or moving with pauses or stops, uncertain or hesitant.
vocabulary.example: Her halting speech betrayed her nervousness.

vocabulary.definition: Walked with difficulty, typically because of pain or injury.
vocabulary.example: He hobbled after the dog on his injured leg.

vocabulary.definition: A series of words said as a magic spell or charm.
vocabulary.example: The wizard muttered an incantation to cast a spell.

vocabulary.definition: A round handle, typically on a door or drawer.
vocabulary.example: She turned the door knob and entered.

vocabulary.definition: Houses that have been decorated with paint, often brightly coloured.
vocabulary.example: The street was lined with colourful painted houses.

vocabulary.definition: An annoying or harmful insect or animal that damages crops, food, or property.
vocabulary.example: Farmers use pesticides to control pests.

vocabulary.definition: To depend on someone or something for support or help.
vocabulary.example: You can rely on her to get the job done.

vocabulary.definition: The inability to rest or relax due to worry or boredom.
vocabulary.example: He was filled with restlessness during the long wait.

vocabulary.definition: Travelled or wandered over an area.
vocabulary.example: He roved through the fields all afternoon.

vocabulary.definition: Wandering from place to place without a fixed destination.
vocabulary.example: The roving reporter traveled across the country.

vocabulary.definition: A small piece or amount of something.
vocabulary.example: She saved every scrap of fabric for her patchwork quilt.

vocabulary.definition: Made marks or wrote something quickly and untidily.
vocabulary.example: He scribbled down a quick note before leaving.

vocabulary.definition: Moving fast in a straight line, especially clouds or waves driven by the wind.
vocabulary.example: Clouds were scudding rapidly across the sky.

vocabulary.definition: Done too quickly and carelessly.
vocabulary.example: He admitted to being slapdash in his work.

vocabulary.definition: To try to find out about someone's private affairs in a sneaky way.
vocabulary.example: She snooped through his letters.

vocabulary.definition: To exchange one thing for another.
vocabulary.example: They swapped seats so that he could sit closer to the window.
